seo_title: Persistent AI Agents in Academic Research: A Single-Investigator…
meta_description: A single-investigator implementation case study on persistent human-agent environments, exploring the economic unit shift from cost per token to cost per…
body_html:
The study found that persistent agentic environments may shift the economic unit from cost per token to cost per completed artifact.

A structured self-observed implementation case study was conducted from January 31 to May 25, 2026. The unit of analysis was the persistent human-agent environment: researcher, agent runtime, memory layer, tools, repositories, scheduled jobs, specialized agent roles, and governance rules.

Recoverable Main-Agent Telemetry Total records across 96 active days 75,671 de-duplicated records

User-Role and Assistant-Role Messages Total messages 8,059 user-role and 23,710 assistant-role messages

Memory-Related Files Total files in the workspace 502 memory-related files

Cached Model-Completed Events Total events with cache reads 73.95 million recorded tokens, 82.9% of which were cache reads

Failure and Verification Events Total failure, verification, correction, or protocol-proxy events 889 failure, verification, correction, or protocol-proxy events

Active System Time Total active system time 579.7 hours (30-minute capped-gap estimate)

Workflow Dominance Dominant workflow type Cached-dominant workflow

Conclusion and Future Directions Future evaluations should use artifact-level denominators, reproducible parsing rules, correction taxonomies, and independent coding of governance events.

source.title: Persistent AI Agents in Academic Research: A Single-Investigator Implementation Case Study
source.summary: Researchers conducted a single-investigator case study to investigate the behavior of an AI agent embedded in a real academic research environment with persistent memory and local files. The study collected telemetry data from January 31 to May 25, 2026, covering various aspects of the agent's performance, including architecture, utilization, artifact production, and governance. The results showed that the workflow was cache-dominant, suggesting a shift in the economic unit from cost per token to cost per completed artifact.
